user_query:
Please set up a small event-triggered local automation that watches the ./incoming directory for new or modified files and performs two actions, then run it once in one-shot mode to process the provided samples.

Requirements:
1) Manuscript citation normalization (trigger: a new or updated *.md file in ./incoming):
- Parse in-text citations in the manuscript that use Pandoc-style markers like [@key] or clusters [@key1; @key2].
- Build a citation number map by order of first appearance in the manuscript text (1..N). Replace each in-text marker with bracketed numeric citations using that mapping (e.g., [1] or [1, 3]); sort numbers ascending within clusters and do not condense into ranges.
- Ignore any existing "References" section in the manuscript and regenerate it from scratch using input/bibliography.json. Include only keys that appear in the manuscript, sorted by their assigned number. Preserve the rest of the manuscript text and headings.
- Produce these outputs:
  - out/manuscript_draft.normalized.md: the edited manuscript with numeric citations and a regenerated References section.
  - out/citation_map.json: a JSON object mapping each cited key to its assigned number (e.g., {"smith2019": 1, ...}).
  - out/references.csv: a CSV with header number,key,authors,title,journal,year,volume,issue,pages,doi and one row per cited item, sorted by number.

2) Cover email polishing (trigger: a new or updated plain-text file matching email_to_editor*.txt in ./incoming):
- Rewrite the email to a concise (<=120 words) professional tone. Replace placeholders in double braces (e.g., {{MANUSCRIPT_TITLE}}, {{JOURNAL}}, {{corresponding_author}}, {{affiliation}}, {{manuscript_type}}) with values from input/metadata.yaml.
- Ensure the output begins with a Subject: line (reuse the existing one if present, otherwise create: "Subject: Submission: <MANUSCRIPT_TITLE> to <JOURNAL>"). End the body with a sign-off that includes the corresponding author's name and affiliation.
- Produce this output:
  - out/email_to_editor.polished.txt: the rewritten email with placeholders resolved and word count <=120 for the body.

Automation details:
- Implement the automation as a command-line script at tools/watch_and_process.py that watches ./incoming for created/modified files and applies the above transforms. Provide a --once flag that processes any current files in ./incoming immediately and exits (sufficient for verification here). Do not perform any network calls; use only the provided local inputs.
- After implementing, run: python tools/watch_and_process.py --once so that the provided samples are processed and the outputs are generated under ./out as specified.

Input paths:
- Manuscript: incoming/manuscript_draft.md
- Bibliography: input/bibliography.json
- Email draft: incoming/email_to_editor.txt
- Metadata for placeholders: input/metadata.yaml

Deliverables that must exist after running in one-shot mode:
- tools/watch_and_process.py
- out/manuscript_draft.normalized.md
- out/citation_map.json
- out/references.csv
- out/email_to_editor.polished.txt All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
